items.title: Roadmap: From Where You Are to Expert (DSA + System Design)
items.summary: You have ~63 KB of DSA curriculum + a system design curriculum + a tradeoffs report, all sitting in a folder. None of that is progress. Progress = problems solved + designs explained out loud.

anchors.text: ❌ What NOT to Do at This Level

items.summary: By the end of Phase 1 you'll be able to: - Explain DNS, HTTP, TCP, UDP, load balancers, and CDN like a pro - Reason about vertical vs horizontal scaling and stateless services - Choose between SQL and NoSQL with sound…

items.title: 📗 Phase 2: Intermediate — Designing Scalable Systems

anchors.text: ✅ How to Know You're Ready for Phase 3

items.summary: By the end of Phase 2 you'll be able to: - Design Twitter, WhatsApp, Uber, and YouTube at a credible level - Choose data models, partitioning strategies, and caching layers deliberately - Reason about trade-offs in…

items.title: 📕 Phase 3: Advanced — Distributed Systems Theory & Practice

anchors.text: 📄 Seminal Papers — The Required Reading List
Goal A: Implement Raft (the rite of passage)
Goal B: Build a Mini Distributed KV Store
Goal C: Contribute to a Distributed System
✅ How to Know You're Ready for Phase 4

items.summary: This is where you stop being someone who uses distributed systems and start being someone who understands them.

items.summary: There's no graduation. Expertise compounds with every system you ship and every post-mortem you read.

items.summary: There is exactly one correct answer to almost every system-design interview question, and it is "it depends — on what?" The job of a senior engineer is not to know which technology is "best." It is to identify the two…

items.title: PART 1 — How to Think About Trade-offs

anchors.text: Model 1: Two-way doors vs one-way doors (Bezos, 2015 shareholder letter)
Model 6: The Constant Work Pattern (AWS Builders' Library)
1.2 The Three Questions to Ask Before Any Decision

items.summary: When you say "it depends," always answer the follow-up before it's asked. The variables that almost every architecture decision turns on:

items.title: PART 2 — HLD Trade-offs — Part 1: 2A. Foundational Theorems → 2B. Data Tier Trade-offs

anchors.text: Cache Invalidation: The Real Hard Problem
Cache Eviction: LRU / LFU / ARC / TinyLFU
Message Queues: Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS vs Pulsar vs NATS
Coordination Avoidance / I-Confluence (Bailis et al., VLDB 2015)

items.summary: In the presence of a network Partition, you must choose between Consistency (all nodes see the same data) and Availability (every request gets a response).

items.title: PART 2 — HLD Trade-offs — Part 2: 2C. Architecture Pattern Trade-offs → 2E. Operations Trade-offs

anchors.text: Monolith vs Microservices vs Modular Monolith
REST vs gRPC vs GraphQL vs WebSockets

items.summary: Split write model from read model. Writes go to a normalized OLTP store; reads go to denormalized projections optimized for queries.

items.title: ⚡ 5-Minute Reads — System Design Quick Primers
items.summary: Each read includes: - 🎯 TL;DR — the one-line takeaway - 📖 Plain English explanation - 🔑 Key trade-offs - 🏗️ Real-world example - 🚦 When to use / when NOT to use - 🔗 Where to dig deeper

items.title: 🟢 #1 — What is System Design? (5-min read)
items.summary: When you write a small program, you're solving a problem. When you do system design, you're solving a problem and answering questions like:

items.title: 🟢 #2 — Scalability: Vertical vs Horizontal (5-min read)

anchors.text: ⚙️ Stateless vs Stateful — The Critical Concept

items.summary: Your app is becoming popular. Two roads to handle the traffic:

anchors.text: 🏗️ Latency Numbers Every Engineer Should Know

items.summary: Imagine a coffee shop:

items.title: 🟢 #4 — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC (5-min read)
items.summary: When two computers talk to each other over the network, they need a protocol. Three popular choices for application APIs:

items.title: 🟢 #5 — SQL vs NoSQL (5-min read)
items.summary: These have almost nothing in common with each other except "not SQL."

items.title: 🟡 #6 — Load Balancing (5-min read)

anchors.text: 🏗️ Layer 4 vs Layer 7 — THE Critical Distinction

items.summary: You have 10 backend servers. A user sends a request. Where does it go?

items.title: 🟡 #7 — Caching Strategies (5-min read)
items.summary: Your database query takes 50ms. Same query 1 million times = a slow service.

items.title: 🟡 #8 — CDN: Content Delivery Networks (5-min read)
items.summary: Your web server is in Virginia. A user in Tokyo loads your site. The packets must cross the Pacific (≈150ms each way). Multiply by 50 assets (CSS, JS, images) and the page is slow.

items.title: 🟡 #9 — Database Replication (5-min read)
items.summary: You have one database. Two problems:

items.title: 🟡 #10 — Database Sharding (5-min read)

anchors.text: 🔑 Choosing a Shard Key — The #1 Decision

items.summary: Replication gave us many copies of the same data — great for reads, useless for writes. All writes still go to one primary.

items.title: 🟡 #11 — Consistent Hashing (5-min read)

anchors.text: The Naïve Approach (and why it's broken)

items.summary: You have 4 cache servers. You assign keys: server = hash(key) % 4.

items.title: 🟡 #12 — Microservices vs Monolith (5-min read)

anchors.text: 🪜 The Modular Monolith — The Pragmatic Middle

items.summary: A monolith has nasty in-codebase complexity: tangled modules, shared dependencies, slow build times, fear of changing anything.

items.title: 🟡 #13 — Message Queues & Pub/Sub (5-min read)

anchors.text: Pub/Sub (Topic-Based) — every subscriber gets a copy
📊 Big Three: Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS

items.summary: A user uploads a video on YouTube. What happens?

items.title: 🟡 #14 — Rate Limiting (5-min read)

anchors.text: Option 3: Sticky routing (LB hashes user_id to one server)

items.summary: Without rate limiting: - A buggy client retries forever in a tight loop → DDoS yourself - A malicious bot scrapes your API → cost explosion - One customer accidentally floods → harms all other customers

items.title: 🔴 #15 — The CAP Theorem (5-min read)

anchors.text: 🎯 CP vs AP — A Concrete Example
2. CAP is not about all consistency
3. CAP doesn't apply to single-node systems
4. CAP doesn't tell you the latency cost

items.summary: CAP says: a distributed system can guarantee at most two of:

items.title: 🔴 #16 — Consistency Models (5-min read)
items.summary: When two people read the same data on different servers, what should they see?

items.title: 🔴 #17 — Consensus: Raft & Paxos (5-min read)
items.summary: You have 5 servers. They need to agree: "what's the next entry in our log?"

items.title: 🔴 #18 — Event-Driven Architecture & Saga Pattern (5-min read)

anchors.text: 🌀 The Saga Pattern — Distributed Transactions Without 2PC

items.summary: If any service is slow or down, the whole chain fails.

items.title: 🔴 #19 — Resilience Patterns: Circuit Breaker & Bulkhead (5-min read)

anchors.text: 🌐 Service Mesh: All of This, For Free

items.summary: You have 10 microservices. Service B starts responding slowly. Service A calls B, A's threads pile up waiting. Service C calls A, C's threads pile up waiting. The whole system grinds to a halt.

items.title: 🔴 #20 — Observability: Logs, Metrics, Traces (5-min read)

anchors.text: 🚦 Day-One Setup for a New Service
